Summary Statement.comment: Mention how long pain lasts when it comes (has it been constant for months, vs. episodic lasting seconds/minutes/hours?  What medication has it been refarctory to/?

Differential Diagnosis.comment: Nice discussion and listing of probability

Plan.comment: Be a little more specific about what you are looking for (e.g. an EKG will likely not show you stable angina if it is obtained while the patient is pain-free).

Summary Statement.comment: Refine description of time course - is it continuous vs episodic, and do the episodes last seconds/minutes/hours?

Differential Diagnosis.comment: Always consider a more serious diagnosis, even if it seems less likely - this person has cardiac risk factors and might have cardiac ischemia.

Plan.comment:
As above, need to consider cardiac disease as well.
Endoscopy can also look for signs of/complications of GERD.

Summary Statement.comment: Refine description of time course - is it continuous or episodic, and do episodes last seconds, minutes, hours, days?

Differential Diagnosis.comment: Overall good - Do you think patient is having an MI right now, or more likely angina?

Explanation of Alternative Diagnoses.comment: I don't think he has a "cardiac history".  Story not consistent with pancreatitis.

Plan.comment: Always look for a more serious diagnosis, even if it seems less likely - this person has cardiac risk factors and might have cardiac ischemia

Summary Statement.comment: Be more concise in the summary.  Refine description of time course - is it continuous or episodic, and do episodes last seconds, minutes, hours, days?

Differential Diagnosis.comment: Nice listing and rationale of your differential

Explanation of Alternative Diagnoses.comment: The physical exam findings you list are mostly associated with CHF, not with CAD in the absence of CHF.

Plan.comment:
EKG and troponin don't really assess "cardiac function" (an echo would be better for that) - EKG and troponin are looking for an acute MI, which might or might not be the type of cardiac ischemia this patient has
.

Summary Statement.comment: Summary should be more concise.  Refine description of time course - is it continuous or episodic, and do episodes last seconds, minutes, hours, days?

Differential Diagnosis.comment: Either PUD or GERD could respond or not respond to appropriate meds, so not really a differentiating factor.  You are correct that S4 is thought to be due to ventricular stiffness, often due to hypertension.  The things that make coronary disease more likely are the patient's risk factors.

Explanation of Alternative Diagnoses.comment: S4 is thought to be due to a stiff heart from any cause (likely hypertension in his case)

Plan.comment: Always look into a more serious diagnosis, even if it seems less likely - this person has cardiac risk factors and might have cardiac ischemia.

Summary Statement.comment: Not sure you got the same case as everyone else.

Differential Diagnosis.comment: Sounds like you failed to elicit the history of chest pain.

Plan.comment: as above

Summary Statement.comment: Mention both cardiac and GI risk factors.  Refine description of time course - is it continuous or episodic, and do episodes last seconds, minutes, hours, days?

Differential Diagnosis.comment:
H pylori doesn't lead to GERD (it can lead to peptic ulcer disease).  
Always consider a more serious diagnosis, even if it seems less likely - this person has cardiac risk factors and might have cardiac ischemia.

Plan.comment: Tell the reader what you are doing and why, and as above always consider the most severe thing on a reasonable differential.

The endoscopy could look for the other GI things on your list, too.
Always look into a more serious diagnosis, even if it seems less likely - this person has cardiac risk factors and might have cardiac ischemia.

Summary Statement.comment: Mention risk factors for cardiac and GI disease.  Refine description of time course - is it continuous or episodic, and do episodes last seconds, minutes, hours, days?

Plan.comment: Always look into a more serious diagnosis, even if it seems less likely - this person has cardiac risk factors and might have cardiac ischemia.

Summary Statement.comment: Minor omission - would mention that frequency/severity is increasing over the past month or so.

Differential Diagnosis.comment: Very nice discussion or symptoms and your thought process.

Plan.comment:
Minor criticism - a chest Xray can't "rule out" aortic dissection.  It can make it less likely, but a more sensitive test is needed if you really think a person might (>5% chance or so) have aortic dissection.
Overall, nicely thought out plan to treat for and look into the GI illnesses you think are most likely, and look for the more dangerous and possible diagnosis of cardiac ischemic.

Summary Statement.comment: Summary should be more concise.  Refine description of time course - is it continuous or episodic, and do episodes last seconds, minutes, hours, days?

Differential Diagnosis.comment: Concise discussion of your thought process.  Could the patient have other GI illnesses?  What type of angina do you think they might have (ACS or stable)?

Plan.comment:
Your leading diagnosis is GERD - what will you do if the treatment continues to fail?
Good idea to do a stress test to look for the more serious diagnosis, even if it seems less likely.

Summary Statement.comment: Summary should be more concise.  Refine description of time course - is it continuous or episodic, and do episodes last seconds, minutes, hours, days?

Plan.comment: Always address a more serious diagnosis, even if it seems less likely - this person has cardiac risk factors and might have cardiac ischemia.

Summary Statement.comment: Summary should be more concise.  Refine description of time course - is it continuous or episodic, and do episodes last seconds, minutes, hours, days?

Differential Diagnosis.comment:
Agree that it is very important to look for possibility of myocardial ischemia.
Of the GI diagnoses, pancreatitis should NOT be you number one choice - it usually causes prolonged/persistent rather than episodic pain.  The pain pattern described is much more typical of GERD, which does not always respond to appropriate medication.  Abdominal bruits are a very insensitive indicator of vascular disease, so their absence is not very useful.

Explanation of Lead Diagnosis.comment: S4 is thought to be related to cardiac stiffness of any cause, not just MI.

Explanation of Alternative Diagnoses.comment: Story not very consistent with pancreatitis, much more likely GI diagnosis is GERD resistant to meds.

Plan.comment: As above, the mostly likely cardiac diagnosis is not a current acute MI, and the mostly likely GI diagnosis is not pancreatitis.

Summary Statement.comment: Refine description of time course - is it continuous or episodic, and do episodes last seconds, minutes, hours, days?

Differential Diagnosis.comment: Good description of the items making these diagnoses more or less likely.

Plan.comment: Succinct, appropriate plan.

Summary Statement.comment: Minor criticism - Refine description of time course - do episodes last seconds, minutes, hours?

Differential Diagnosis.comment: Clear statement of your thgouth process

Explanation of Lead Diagnosis.comment: Good thought process but be more explicit.  What about the history and physical make you think GERD (I agree with you)?

Plan.comment: Labs and EKG might or might not show angina, especially if done while the patient is pain-free.  Be more clear about how one would "confirm" GERD.

Summary Statement.comment: Refine description of time course -  do episodes last seconds, minutes, hours, days?

Differential Diagnosis.comment: Clear explanation of your thought process

Do you think the patient has acute ischemia at this very moment or a more intermittent, stable type of cardiac ischemia?

How good is an EKG at looking of coronary disease in general?
What will you do if medical management continues to fail?

Summary Statement.comment: Very nice thought process.  Summary should be more succinct..

Differential Diagnosis.comment: Really excellent discussion clarifying your thought process about the probability of these various diagnoses.

Explanation of Alternative Diagnoses.comment: Overall this is a bit too lengthy - could get the important points across more succinctly.

Plan.comment: Very complete plan including preliminary and follow-up steps.  Quite nice

Differential Diagnosis.comment: Very nice and clear description of your thought process

Plan.comment:
Good to address most serious diagnosis even if not the most likely.
What will you do if PPI doesn't lead to resolution?

Summary Statement.comment: Refine description of time course - is it continuous or episodic, and do episodes last seconds, minutes, hours, days?

Differential Diagnosis.comment: Minor criticisms - H pylori can occur in the absence of PUD, and vice versa.  I think the patient does have chest pain (burning would be a subcategory of pain) - so I didn't understand your final comment.

Explanation of Alternative Diagnoses.comment: Mention risk factors and features making GERD more likely than CAD.

Plan.comment: An EKG is often normal in patient with coronary disease, especially if the EKG is done while the patient is pain-free - so an EKG cannot be said to "rule out" cardiac issues.  As above,  H pylori can occur in the absence of PUD, and vice versa.

Summary Statement.comment: Refine description of time course - is it continuous or episodic, and do episodes last seconds, minutes, hours, days?

Differential Diagnosis.comment: Would explain your thought process - what goes for, what goes against

Plan.comment: Needs to be fleshed out.  How good is an EKG for looking for the type of cardiac ischemia you think the patient has? (not very, unless currently having an acute MI) What other cardiac work-up, therapy might one do?  What would you do about a possible GI illness?

Summary Statement.comment: Mention more cardiac and/or GI risk factors.  Refine description of time course - is it continuous or episodic, and do episodes last seconds, minutes, hours, days?

Differential Diagnosis.comment:
Always consider a more serious disease, even if it's not the most likely one.  This person has cardiac risk factors and might have cardiac ischemia.
Minor criticism - eosiniphilic esophagitis is usually a chronic illness also.

Explanation of Alternative Diagnoses.comment: Would also consider possible cardiac causes given the risk factors for same.

Summary Statement.comment: Minor criticism - Refine description of time course - do episodes last seconds, minutes, hours, days?

Differential Diagnosis.comment: Overall good but could flesh out pro-cons a bit more.

Plan.comment: Overall good plan.  EKG and troponins sensitive for MI/ACS, but not if the symptoms are not currently present when the test is done, or no prior transmural MI.

Summary Statement.comment: Good content, but summary should be more succinct.

Differential Diagnosis.comment: Esophagitis often accompanies GERD, so your last sentence isn't useful.

Plan.comment: EKG is unlikely to find cardiac ischemia unless the patient is having pain while the test is being done.

Summary Statement.comment: Refine description of time course - is it continuous or episodic, and do episodes last seconds, minutes, hours, days?

Differential Diagnosis.comment:
She might also have a GI illness such as GERD, with a high enough likelihood to deserve a place on the list.
Pericarditis tends to be a more persistent pain and pleuritic in nature, so not too likely.

Explanation of Alternative Diagnoses.comment: Need to consider GI disease as an alternative

Plan.comment: How good is an EKG done while pain-free to look for stable angina?  (not very sensitive)  Try to be more specific about what you are looking for in the different conditions - e.g. ST changes in the infarct area for MI, global ST elevation for pericarditis, and probably normal in stable angina.  THen would need further testing.

Summary Statement.comment: Very good content, but summary should be more concise.  Refine description of time course - is it continuous or episodic, and do episodes last seconds, minutes, hours, days?

Differential Diagnosis.comment: Clear description of your thought process.

Plan.comment: Agree that trop/EKG would be useful and important to ensure no ACS, but not so great to look for stable angina (which I agree with you is most likely cardiac diagnosis).

Summary Statement.comment: Refine description of time course - is it continuous or episodic, and do episodes last seconds, minutes, hours, days?

Differential Diagnosis.comment: Some of the complaints suggest a GI, likely esophageal disorder, so would include this on your differential

Plan.comment: For suspected ACS, EKG and troponin would be analyzed first, and cath lab eval only if they are suggestive of acute injury.

Summary Statement.comment: Mention cardiac and GI risk factors.  Refine description of time course -do episodes last seconds, minutes, hours?

Plan.comment: Good eval for stable coronary disease.  Would also address possibility of GI disease.
